insights.title: Bank credit at ₹215.2L Cr — new all-time high
insights.body: Total bank credit outstanding reached ₹215.2L Cr in May 2026, the highest level on record. Volume rose ₹3.0L Cr period-on-period (₹212.1L Cr → ₹215.2L Cr), reversing April's post-year-end dip and resuming the climb from ₹178.7L Cr in January 2025.
insights.implication: The credit cycle is back on track. Lenders should prepare for sustained high-volume deployment through FY27 — this is a growth environment, not a consolidation phase. Watch for capacity constraints and asset quality pressure as the system scales.

inferences: Bank credit hit ₹215.2L Cr in May 2026, up ₹3.0L Cr from April's ₹212.1L Cr.
April's decline was a temporary correction; May's rebound confirms the underlying expansion trajectory remains intact.
The system has added ₹36.5L Cr since January 2025, with no sign of structural slowdown.

insights.title: Bank credit grew 10.0% in FY25, 17.1% in FY26 — 7.1pp step-up
insights.body: Bank credit grew 10.0% in FY25 and 17.1% in FY26, a 7.1 percentage point acceleration. The full-year growth rate jumped from 10.0% in the year ending March 2025 to 17.1% in the year ending March 2026, the largest annual step-up in this dataset.
insights.implication: The credit cycle has turned decisively. Banks and NBFCs that built capacity early are winning share; laggards face permanent disadvantage. Expect FY27 to sustain this pace unless macro headwinds emerge.

inferences: Bank credit grew 10.0% in FY25 and 17.1% in FY26, a 7.1 percentage point acceleration.
This is the sharpest year-over-year step-up recorded, signaling a decisive shift in the credit cycle.
Lenders that scaled underwriting and distribution in FY25 are now capturing compounding gains in FY26.

insights.title: Banks deployed ₹31.2L Cr in FY26 — record annual add
insights.body: Banks deployed ₹31.2L Cr of net new credit in FY26, the highest annual addition in this window. This figure has remained constant at ₹31.2L Cr across all nine periods, as it reflects the full-year tally locked at March 2026 year-end.
insights.implication: The system added more credit in one year than in any prior period tracked. Lenders must audit asset quality and capital adequacy headroom — growth at this scale demands heightened risk discipline.

inferences: Banks added ₹31.2L Cr of net new credit in FY26, the largest single-year deployment on record.
This volume exceeds any prior annual addition tracked, signaling aggressive system-wide expansion.
Growth at this velocity can mask emerging stress in weaker pockets or over-leveraged segments.

insights.title: Bank credit YoY at 17.7% — re-accelerating to record high
insights.body: Bank credit grew 17.7% year-on-year in May 2026, the highest growth rate in this dataset. Growth re-accelerated 1.6 percentage points (16.0% → 17.7%), reversing April's post-year-end slowdown and surpassing March's 17.1% peak.
insights.implication: April's slowdown was noise, not signal. Credit is expanding faster than at any point in this dataset. Lenders face intense competition for quality borrowers — expect pricing pressure, margin compression, and heightened underwriting discipline to sustain portfolio quality.

inferences: Bank credit grew 17.7% year-on-year in May 2026, up from 16.0% in April 2026.
April's deceleration was a temporary normalization; May's re-acceleration confirms the underlying momentum is intact.
Growth is now at the highest level recorded in this nine-period window, above the 16.5% upper range threshold.

insights.title: Food credit surged to ₹1.37L Cr — new all-time high
insights.body: Food credit outstanding jumped to ₹1.37L Cr in May 2026, the highest level on record. Volume rose ₹0.33L Cr period-on-period (₹1.04L Cr → ₹1.37L Cr), the second consecutive large increase and a new all-time high.
insights.implication: Food credit is a rounding error in the system, but the two-period surge signals active procurement financing. Lenders in agri-commodity finance should watch for repayment cycles as the season progresses.

inferences: Food credit hit ₹1.37L Cr in May 2026, up ₹0.33L Cr from April's ₹1.04L Cr.
This is the second straight period of sharp increases, following April's ₹0.34L Cr jump from March.
Food credit remains a small fraction of the ₹215L Cr system, but the surge signals active procurement financing.

insights.title: Food credit YoY at 94.1% — back near record high
insights.body: Food credit grew 94.1% year-on-year in May 2026, near the highest growth rate in this dataset. Growth re-accelerated 27.7 percentage points (66.4% → 94.1%), reversing April's sharp deceleration and returning to the upper range near February's 94.2% peak.
insights.implication: The triple-digit growth rates are compressing as the base normalizes, but food credit remains robust in absolute terms. Expect further rate volatility in coming periods as the low base from early 2025 rolls off.

inferences: Food credit grew 94.1% year-on-year in May 2026, up from 66.4% in April 2026.
April's deceleration was a temporary compression; May's re-acceleration confirms the underlying surge is intact.
Growth is now back near the 94.2% upper range threshold, driven by a low base from May 2025.

insights.title: Non-food credit at ₹213.8L Cr — new all-time high
insights.body: Non-food credit outstanding reached ₹213.8L Cr in May 2026, the highest level on record. Volume rose ₹2.7L Cr period-on-period (₹211.1L Cr → ₹213.8L Cr), reversing April's post-year-end dip and resuming the climb from ₹178.1L Cr in January 2025.
insights.implication: Non-food credit is where the real lending action is. The May rebound confirms April's dip was a post-year-end correction, not a trend reversal. Lenders should expect sustained high-volume deployment across agriculture, industry, services, and retail.

inferences: Non-food credit hit ₹213.8L Cr in May 2026, up ₹2.7L Cr from April's ₹211.1L Cr.
April's decline was a temporary correction; May's rebound confirms the underlying expansion trajectory remains intact.
Non-food credit drives the system-wide trend, accounting for 99.4% of total bank credit.

insights.title: Non-food credit added ₹30.8L Cr in FY26 — record annual deployment
insights.body: Banks deployed ₹30.8L Cr of net new non-food credit in FY26, the highest annual addition in this window. This figure has remained constant at ₹30.8L Cr across all nine periods, as it reflects the full-year tally locked at March 2026 year-end.
insights.implication: Non-food credit is where the real lending action is. The ₹30.8L Cr add signals aggressive deployment across productive sectors, but lenders must watch for pockets of over-leverage as growth sustains at this scale.

inferences: Banks added ₹30.8L Cr of net new non-food credit in FY26, the largest single-year deployment on record.
This volume accounts for 98.9% of the total ₹31.2L Cr system-wide addition, confirming non-food credit as the primary growth driver.
Growth at this scale demands heightened risk discipline to avoid pockets of over-leverage.

insights.title: Non-food credit YoY at 17.4% — re-accelerating to record high
insights.body: Non-food credit grew 17.4% year-on-year in May 2026, the highest growth rate in this dataset. Growth re-accelerated 1.5 percentage points (15.8% → 17.4%), reversing April's post-year-end slowdown and surpassing March's 16.9% peak.
insights.implication: April's slowdown was noise, not signal. Non-food credit is expanding faster than at any point in this dataset. Lenders should expect pricing pressure and margin compression in competitive segments as growth sustains above historical norms.

inferences: Non-food credit grew 17.4% year-on-year in May 2026, up from 15.8% in April 2026.
April's deceleration was a temporary normalization; May's re-acceleration confirms the underlying momentum is intact.
Growth is now at the highest level recorded in this nine-period window, above the 16.4% upper range threshold.

insights.title: All 4 main sectors growing — breadth at maximum
insights.body: All four main credit sectors — agriculture, industry, services, retail — posted positive year-on-year growth in May 2026. Breadth has held at 4 out of 4 sectors for the entire nine-period window, with no sector slipping into contraction.
insights.implication: Credit expansion is firing on all cylinders. Lenders face intense competition for quality borrowers in every sector — expect pricing pressure, margin compression, and heightened underwriting discipline to sustain portfolio quality.

inferences: All four main credit sectors posted positive year-on-year growth in May 2026, maintaining full breadth.
This is the ninth consecutive period with all sectors growing, the longest streak on record.
Broad-based expansion signals synchronized demand across agriculture, industry, services, and retail.

insights.title: Agriculture share steady at 12.36% — stable allocation
insights.body: Agriculture's share of total bank credit stands at 12.36% in May 2026. Share declined 0.09pp from 12.44% last period, holding near the median of its nine-period range (12.3%–12.5%).
insights.implication: Agriculture maintains a stable 12.4% footprint in the credit mix — proportional growth signals balanced portfolio expansion. Lenders can sustain current allocation without structural rebalancing pressure.

inferences: Agriculture accounts for 12.36% of total bank credit, down 0.09pp from 12.44% last period.
The share has oscillated within a narrow 0.2pp band (12.3%–12.5%) across nine periods, with no sustained directional trend.
This stability indicates agriculture is growing in line with the overall credit pool, neither gaining nor losing structural ground.

insights.title: Agriculture credit at 14.91% YoY — re-accelerating after Apr dip
insights.body: Agriculture credit grew 14.91% YoY in May 2026, up from 13.70% last period. Growth re-accelerated 1.21pp from 13.70% to 14.91%, reversing the prior period's 1.92pp deceleration from the Mar 2026 peak of 15.62%. The trajectory shows: 13.7% → 14.9%.
insights.implication: Agriculture lending has resumed its strong trajectory after a one-period dip — the 14.91% rate confirms robust demand, likely driven by Kharif season credit and government-backed schemes. Lenders should maintain exposure; the re-acceleration signals sustained momentum, not a temporary spike.

inferences: Agriculture credit grew 14.91% YoY in May 2026, up 1.21pp from 13.70% last period.
The prior period saw a 1.92pp deceleration from the Mar 2026 peak of 15.62%; this period reverses that softening.
The current rate of 14.91% sits above the upper range (p75=14.3%), confirming momentum has returned after a brief pause.

insights.title: Industry credit grew 8.2% → 15.0% — 6.8pp step-up
insights.body: Industry credit grew 8.2% in FY25 and 15.0% in FY26, marking a 6.8-point acceleration. The full-year acceleration, measured at Mar 2026 year-end, reflects a near-doubling of annual growth momentum from the prior fiscal year.
insights.implication: Industry credit has broken out of its multi-year slowdown — the 6.8pp acceleration confirms a capex-driven recovery. Lenders should prioritize sectors with strong order books, balance sheet repair, and PLI-linked demand. This is the most compelling industrial lending opportunity in years.

inferences: Industry credit grew 8.2% in FY25 and 15.0% in FY26, a 6.8-point step-up.
This acceleration is the largest in the dataset, signaling a structural shift from multi-year slowdown to capex-driven recovery.
The 15.0% FY26 rate is the highest annual growth in recent years, confirming the industrial lending cycle has turned.

insights.title: Industry share at 21.63% — edging up from range low
insights.body: Industry accounts for 21.63% of total bank credit in May 2026. Share rose 0.13pp from 21.50% last period, climbing from the lower bound of its range (21.5%–21.9%) toward the median.
insights.implication: Industry is in a strong growth phase but not displacing retail or services as the dominant driver. Lenders can lean into industrial exposure for growth, but the sector is not yet commanding a larger slice of the pool. Watch for sustained share gains above 21.9% as a signal of structural rebalancing.

inferences: Industry's share stands at 21.63%, up 0.13pp from 21.50% last period.
The prior period marked the lowest level in this nine-period window; this period shows a modest recovery.
Despite strong YoY growth (17.55%), industry is not yet gaining structural share — it is recovering, not reshaping the credit mix.

insights.title: Industry credit at 17.55% YoY — highest on record
insights.body: Industry credit grew 17.55% YoY in May 2026, the fastest rate in this nine-period window. Growth accelerated 2.49pp from 15.06% to 17.55%, breaking above the prior maximum of 15.62%. The trajectory shows sustained acceleration: 15.0% → 15.1% → 17.5%.
insights.implication: Industry lending has reached a new high and is accelerating — the 17.55% pace confirms the capex cycle is strengthening, not plateauing. Lenders should prioritize sectors with strong order books, infrastructure exposure, and balance sheet repair. This is the strongest industrial lending signal in years; any deceleration below 15% would mark a trend break.

inferences: Industry credit grew 17.55% YoY in May 2026, up 2.49pp from 15.06% last period.
This is the highest rate in the nine-period window, surpassing the prior peak of 15.62%.
The sustained acceleration from 8.2% in Mar 2025 to 17.55% in May 2026 confirms a capex-driven recovery is entrenched and gaining momentum.

insights.title: Personal loans at 32.81% — stable near range median
insights.body: Personal loans account for 32.81% of total bank credit in May 2026. Share declined 0.16pp from 32.98% last period, slipping from above the 75th percentile (32.9%) back toward the median (32.7%–32.9%).
insights.implication: Personal loans have stabilized near 32.8% after a brief bounce last period — the rebalancing toward productive sectors has paused, but retail is not regaining dominance. Lenders should maintain current allocation; the stable share signals balanced portfolio expansion, not a shift back to retail-heavy portfolios.

inferences: Personal loans hold 32.81% share, down 0.16pp from 32.98% last period.
The prior period saw a 0.38pp jump from the range low (32.59%) to above the 75th percentile; this period reverses half that gain.
The share remains near the median of its nine-period range, indicating personal loans are neither gaining nor losing structural ground.

insights.title: Personal loans at 15.38% YoY — easing from Apr high
insights.body: Personal loans credit grew 15.38% YoY in May 2026, down from 16.01% last period. Growth decelerated 0.64pp from 16.01% to 15.38%, slipping from near the upper range (p75=15.7%) toward the median. The trajectory shows: 16.0% → 15.4%.
insights.implication: Personal loans remain a growth engine, sustaining mid-teens expansion, but the deceleration from 16.01% to 15.38% suggests the sector may be plateauing after a strong run. Lenders should monitor unsecured exposure — especially credit cards and personal loans — as growth stabilizes at elevated levels. Watch for further softening below 14% as a signal of peak momentum passing.

inferences: Personal loans grew 15.38% YoY in May 2026, down 0.64pp from 16.01% last period.
The prior period marked the highest rate in this window; this period shows a modest deceleration.
The current rate of 15.38% remains near the median (p25=11.8%, p75=15.7%), indicating personal loans are sustaining mid-teens growth but no longer accelerating.

insights.title: Services credit grew 12.0% → 19.0% — 7.0pp step-up
insights.body: Services credit grew 12.0% in FY25 and 19.0% in FY26, marking a 7.0-point acceleration. The full-year acceleration measured at Mar 2026 year-end is 7.0pp, the sharpest annual step-up in this dataset.
insights.implication: Services credit is in its strongest cycle in recent years — the 7.0-point acceleration confirms a structural shift, not a temporary spike. Lenders should lean into services exposure, particularly NBFCs and trade, while monitoring asset quality as the sector scales rapidly. This is the most compelling services lending opportunity in years.

inferences: Services credit grew 12.0% in FY25 and 19.0% in FY26, a 7.0-point step-up.
This acceleration is the largest in the dataset, signaling a structural shift to a high-growth cycle.
The 19.0% FY26 rate is the highest annual growth in recent years, confirming services credit is in its strongest phase.

insights.title: Services share at 28.28% — holding upper range
insights.body: Services account for 28.28% of total bank credit in May 2026. Share rose 0.07pp from 28.21% last period, holding above the 75th percentile (28.2%) and near the top of the nine-period range (27.8%–28.5%).
insights.implication: Services remain a dominant force in the credit mix, holding near the top of their range. The sector is still capturing a larger slice of the pool than a year ago, but share gains have plateaued. Lenders should maintain services exposure but watch for signs of saturation as share stabilizes around 28.3%.

inferences: Services hold 28.28% share, up 0.07pp from 28.21% last period.
The share remains above the 75th percentile (28.2%) and near the top of the range, indicating services are capturing a larger slice of the pool than a year ago.
Despite strong YoY growth (19.0% in FY26), services are not gaining share aggressively — they are holding structural ground, not reshaping the credit mix.

insights.title: Services credit hit 20.4% YoY — highest on record
insights.body: Services credit grew 20.41% year-on-year in May 2026, the strongest reading in the nine-period dataset. Growth re-accelerated from 18.6% in April 2026 to 20.4% in May 2026, reversing the prior period's deceleration. The trajectory over the past year shows: 12.0% → 15.5% → 16.3% → 19.0% → 18.6% → 20.4%.
insights.implication: Services credit is in a sustained acceleration phase — the April dip was noise, not a trend break. Lenders should lean into Services exposure: the 20.4% growth rate is the highest on record and shows no sign of peaking. Prioritise underwriting in high-velocity sub-sectors (Computer Software, NBFCs, Shipping) where growth is compounding. The next period will test whether this is a new plateau or further acceleration — either way, Services is the sector to overweight.

inferences: Services credit grew 20.41% YoY in May 2026, the highest rate in this dataset and 1.82 points above the prior period's 18.58%.
The April deceleration to 18.6% was a false signal — growth has now broken through the March peak of 19.0% and set a new high.
This re-acceleration confirms Services remains the fastest-growing major sector, with momentum building rather than plateauing.

insights.title: Large corporates hold 67.3% of industry credit — share stable
insights.body: Large corporates account for 67.3% of industry credit in May 2026, up 0.11pp from the prior period. Share compressed from 71.0% in Jan 2025 to 67.3% in May 2026, losing 3.7 percentage points over 16 months.
insights.implication: Large corporates have lost 3.7pp of share but the decline has plateaued. The segment is no longer bleeding share, but it is not reclaiming lost ground either. Lenders overweight to large corporates are capturing a smaller slice of incremental industry credit — the growth opportunity has shifted to MSME borrowers.

inferences: Large corporates hold 67.3% of industry credit in May 2026, down 3.7pp from 71.0% in Jan 2025.
The share has stabilised near 67.2% for the past three periods after a sharp 3.8pp decline through early 2026.
The structural shift is complete — large corporates have ceded share to MSME segments, and the new equilibrium is holding.

insights.title: Large corporate credit at 14.4% YoY — strongest in 16 months
insights.body: Large corporate credit grew 14.4% YoY in May 2026, the strongest reading in this nine-period window. Growth accelerated from 10.0% in Apr 2026 to 14.4%, extending a recovery that began at 5.5% in Jan 2026.
insights.implication: Large corporates are re-accelerating hard, but the 14.4% pace is insufficient to defend market share against 26%+ MSME growth. The segment is healthy and momentum is building, but lenders here are capturing a minority of incremental industry credit. The growth is real, but the structural advantage lies elsewhere.

inferences: Large corporate credit grew 14.4% YoY in May 2026, up 4.34pp from 10.0% last period.
This is the fastest pace in 16 months and marks a sharp re-acceleration after six periods of single-digit growth.
Despite the 14.4% pace, large corporates are still losing share — MSME segments are growing even faster.

insights.title: Medium enterprises hold 9.7% share — four-period plateau continues
insights.body: Medium enterprises account for 9.7% of industry credit in May 2026, down 0.05pp from the prior period. Share rose from 8.9% in Jan 2025 to 9.7% by Jan 2026 and has held at 9.7% for five consecutive periods.
insights.implication: Medium enterprises have carved out a 9.7% share and are defending it. The five-period plateau confirms the segment is capturing steady flow, not outsized growth. Lenders here are riding industry-level growth, not a structural advantage.

inferences: Medium enterprises hold 9.7% of industry credit in May 2026, unchanged for five straight periods.
The segment gained 0.8pp of share from Jan 2025 to Jan 2026, then stabilised.
The five-period plateau signals the segment is growing in line with the broader industry pool, not outpacing it.

insights.title: Medium enterprises at 21.2% YoY — momentum stabilising
insights.body: Medium enterprise credit grew 21.2% YoY in May 2026, up 1.29pp from the prior period. Growth re-accelerated from 19.9% in Apr 2026 to 21.2%, after decelerating from a 22.3% peak in Jan 2026.
insights.implication: Medium enterprises have stabilised at a strong 21% growth rate after a brief deceleration. The segment remains a solid outperformer, but the momentum is no longer building. Lenders should watch for further deceleration — if growth drops below 19%, the segment risks losing share to Micro & Small borrowers.

inferences: Medium enterprise credit grew 21.2% YoY in May 2026, up 1.29pp from 19.9% last period.
The segment had decelerated from 22.3% in Jan 2026 to 19.9% in Apr 2026, but has now stabilised above 21%.
The 21.2% pace is healthy and far ahead of large corporates at 14.4%, but the segment is no longer accelerating.

insights.title: Micro & Small grew 32.7% in FY26 vs 8.9% in FY25 — 23.8pp step-up
insights.body: Micro & Small credit grew 8.9% in the year ending Mar 2025 and 32.7% in the year ending Mar 2026, a 23.8 percentage point acceleration. Full-year growth jumped from 8.9% in FY25 to 32.7% in FY26 — the sharpest year-over-year step-up in this dataset.
insights.implication: This is the defining inflection in industry credit. Micro & Small lending has entered a new growth regime, and lenders with exposure here are riding the fastest-growing segment. The 23.8pp step-up is unprecedented — banks and NBFCs scaling this segment are capturing outsized share of incremental credit.

inferences: Micro & Small credit grew 8.9% in FY25 and 32.7% in FY26, a 23.8pp acceleration.
This is the largest full-year step-up on record, signalling a regime shift in lending to smaller borrowers.
The 32.7% FY26 pace is 3.7× the FY25 rate — this is not a cyclical uptick, it is a structural inflection.

insights.title: Micro & Small hold 23.1% of industry credit — share stable
insights.body: Micro & Small enterprises account for 23.1% of industry credit in May 2026, down 0.06pp from the prior period. Share surged from 20.0% in Mar 2025 to 23.5% in Jan 2026, then eased to 23.1% — a net gain of 3.1 percentage points in 14 months.
insights.implication: Micro & Small borrowers captured 3.1pp of share in 14 months — a structural reallocation from large corporates. The share has now stabilised at 23.1%, suggesting the segment is no longer gaining ground. Lenders focused on large corporates have lost share, but the incremental opportunity in Micro & Small may be moderating.

inferences: Micro & Small enterprises hold 23.1% of industry credit in May 2026, stable for four straight periods.
The segment gained 3.1pp of share from Mar 2025 to Jan 2026, then plateaued.
The four-period plateau at 23.1% signals the segment is growing in line with the broader pool, not outpacing it anymore.

insights.title: Micro & Small at 26.2% YoY — decelerating from 32.7% peak
insights.body: Micro & Small credit grew 26.2% YoY in May 2026, down 3.91pp from the prior period. Growth decelerated from 30.1% in Apr 2026 to 26.2%, after peaking at 32.7% in Mar 2026.
insights.implication: Micro & Small credit is decelerating but remains the hottest segment in industry credit. The 26.2% pace is still strong, but the two-period decline from a 32.7% peak signals momentum is softening. Lenders should watch closely — if growth drops below 25%, the segment risks losing its structural advantage.

inferences: Micro & Small credit grew 26.2% YoY in May 2026, down 3.91pp from 30.1% last period.
The segment has decelerated for two straight periods after holding above 30% for four periods.
Despite the deceleration, the 26.2% pace is still 1.8× the 14.4% pace of large corporates.

insights.title: MSME growing 11.8pp faster than large corporates — spread narrowing
insights.body: MSME credit is growing 11.8 percentage points faster than large corporate credit in May 2026. The spread narrowed from 20.1pp in Apr 2026 to 11.8pp, after widening from 2.1pp in Mar 2025 to 25.7pp in Jan 2026.
insights.implication: The growth divergence between MSME and large corporates is narrowing fast as large corporates re-accelerate and MSME decelerates. The 11.8pp spread is the narrowest since Mar 2025, but MSME segments are still outpacing large corporates by a wide margin. Lenders overweight to large corporates are capturing more incremental credit than before, but the structural advantage remains with MSME borrowers.

inferences: MSME credit is growing 11.8pp faster than large corporate credit in May 2026, down 8.3pp from 20.1pp last period.
The spread has narrowed sharply because large corporates re-accelerated to 14.4% while MSME decelerated to 26.2%.
Despite the narrowing, the 11.8pp spread confirms MSME segments are still capturing the bulk of incremental credit.

insights.title: Housing Finance Companies holds 17.7% of NBFC credit; Public Financial Institutions 17.0%
insights.body: RBI breaks NBFC credit into two sub-categories. Housing Finance Companies holds 17.7% and Public Financial Institutions 17.0% — these are size shares, not growth rates. Together the two break-outs cover 34.7% of NBFC credit; the rest is not sub-classified in the RBI statement.
insights.implication: Read this as size mix, not momentum: the two blocks are close in size — a move in either shifts NBFC credit. Check the matching growth scan before positioning.

inferences: Housing Finance Companies is the larger break-out at 17.7% of NBFC credit.
Public Financial Institutions holds 17.0% — a 0.7 percentage points gap between the only two break-outs.
Together they cover 34.7% of NBFC credit; RBI does not sub-classify the rest.

insights.title: Public Financial Institutions growing at 71.8% YoY; Housing Finance Companies at 19.7%
insights.body: Public Financial Institutions grew 71.8% year-on-year against Housing Finance Companies at 19.7% — a 52.0 percentage points growth gap between the two sub-categories RBI breaks out.
insights.implication: Lenders can lean into Public Financial Institutions (71.8%) and monitor Housing Finance Companies (19.7%). Broad participation supports diversified deployment.

inferences: Public Financial Institutions is the faster of the two at 71.8% YoY.
Housing Finance Companies is at 19.7% — 52.0 percentage points behind.
Both are growing — the gap is about pace, not direction.

insights.title: Non-Banking Financial Companies is the biggest slice of services credit at 34.5%
insights.body: Non-Banking Financial Companies holds the largest share of services credit at 34.5%, Trade 22.5%; Shipping is the smallest at 0.2%. These are size shares, not growth rates. Top three hold 77.3% of services credit, spread 34.4 percentage points.
insights.implication: Composition, not momentum: Non-Banking Financial Companies carries the most weight of services credit. Concentration means the big blocks drive the category — watch them first.

inferences: Non-Banking Financial Companies is the largest block at 34.5% of services credit.
Shipping is the smallest at 0.2% — a 34.4 percentage points spread across 10 sub-categories.
The top three hold 77.3% of services credit — concentrated mix.

insights.title: Computer Software growing fastest at 54.9%; Transport Operators slowest at 7.6%
insights.body: Computer Software leads YoY growth at 54.9%, Shipping at 41.0%; Transport Operators is slowest at 7.6%. 10 of 10 categories growing, spread 47.3 percentage points.
insights.implication: Lenders can lean into Computer Software (54.9%) and monitor Transport Operators (7.6%). Broad participation supports diversified deployment.

inferences: Computer Software is the standout at 54.9% YoY.
Transport Operators is the weakest at 7.6% — a spread of 47.3 percentage points across 10 categories.
10 of 10 categories are growing — broad-based momentum.

insights.title: Wholesale Trade holds 56.4% of trade credit; Retail Trade 43.6%
insights.body: RBI breaks trade credit into two sub-categories. Wholesale Trade holds 56.4% and Retail Trade 43.6% — these are size shares, not growth rates. Together the two break-outs cover 100.0% of trade credit — the full category.
insights.implication: Read this as size mix, not momentum: the two blocks are close in size — a move in either shifts trade credit. Check the matching growth scan before positioning.

inferences: Wholesale Trade is the larger break-out at 56.4% of trade credit.
Retail Trade holds 43.6% — a 12.8 percentage points gap between the only two break-outs.
Together they cover 100.0% of trade credit — the full category.

insights.title: Wholesale Trade growing at 22.3% YoY; Retail Trade at 11.3%
insights.body: Wholesale Trade grew 22.3% year-on-year against Retail Trade at 11.3% — a 10.9 percentage points growth gap between the two sub-categories RBI breaks out.
insights.implication: Lenders can lean into Wholesale Trade (22.3%) and monitor Retail Trade (11.3%). Broad participation supports diversified deployment.

inferences: Wholesale Trade is the faster of the two at 22.3% YoY.
Retail Trade is at 11.3% — 10.9 percentage points behind.
Both are growing — the gap is about pace, not direction.

insights.title: Credit card outstanding at ₹2.95L Cr — down ₹3,481 Cr
insights.body: Credit card outstanding fell to ₹2.95L Cr in May 2026, down ₹3,481 Cr from ₹2.98L Cr in Apr 2026. Volume declined ₹2.98L Cr → ₹2.95L Cr, the second consecutive monthly drop after peaking in Apr 2026.
insights.implication: Credit card portfolios are contracting in absolute terms — lenders are de-risking, not just pausing growth. This is a structural retreat from unsecured lending. Expect continued volume declines through H1 FY27.

inferences: Credit card outstanding fell ₹3,481 Cr to ₹2.95L Cr in May 2026, reversing the Apr 2026 peak of ₹2.98L Cr.
This is the second consecutive monthly decline, following a ₹4,394 Cr drop from Mar to Apr 2026.
The back-to-back declines signal lenders are actively shrinking card portfolios, not just slowing origination.

insights.title: Credit cards below 10% YoY for 7 straight periods — record run
insights.body: Credit card growth has remained below 10% YoY for 7 consecutive periods, the longest such run in this dataset. The sub-10% streak extended from 6 periods in Apr 2026 to 7 periods in May 2026, a new historical maximum.
insights.implication: The 7-period sub-10% streak is now the defining feature of the card market — this is not a pause, it is a new normal. Lenders should abandon volume-driven strategies and focus on yield maximisation and portfolio quality.

inferences: Credit card growth has stayed below 10% YoY for 7 consecutive periods, the longest run on record.
The streak began in Dec 2025 at 1.0% and has persisted through May 2026 at 1.3%, with no period exceeding 3.8%.
This confirms a regime shift from the 10%+ growth rates seen in early 2025 to a sustained low-growth environment.

insights.title: Credit card share at 4.20% — lowest in 9-period window
insights.body: Credit cards now account for 4.20% of personal loans, the smallest share in this 9-period window. Share declined from 4.28% in Apr 2026 to 4.20% in May 2026, continuing the downward trajectory from 5.0% in Jan 2025.
insights.implication: Credit cards are no longer a growth driver for retail lenders — gold and housing are the new engines. This is a permanent shift toward secured lending. Lenders should reallocate capital and origination capacity accordingly.

inferences: Credit card share fell to 4.20% in May 2026, the lowest reading in 9 periods and down 0.8 points from 5.0% in Jan 2025.
The decline is driven by faster growth in secured segments — gold loans and housing are capturing incremental credit flows.
Cards are losing ground structurally within retail credit, not just cyclically.

insights.title: Credit card growth at 1.33% YoY — down 2.5 points from Apr
insights.body: Credit card outstanding grew 1.33% YoY in May 2026, down from 3.78% in Apr 2026. Growth decelerated 3.78% → 1.33%, a 2.45-point drop and the second-lowest reading in this 9-period series.
insights.implication: The Apr 2026 uptick was a false signal — May 2026 confirms the card market remains structurally weak. Lenders should not expect a recovery in FY27. Focus on yield optimisation and risk-adjusted returns, not volume expansion.

inferences: Credit card growth fell to 1.33% YoY in May 2026, down 2.45 points from 3.78% in Apr 2026.
The Apr 2026 uptick to 3.8% was a brief stabilisation, not a recovery — the May 2026 drop confirms the low-growth regime persists.
Growth remains near the bottom of the historical range (p25=1.4%), with no momentum toward double digits.

insights.title: Consumer Durables share at 0.33% — near irrelevance
insights.body: Consumer Durables now represent 0.33% of personal loans, near the median of the 9-period range. Share edged up from 0.32% in Apr 2026 to 0.33% in May 2026, but remains down from 0.4% in early 2025.
insights.implication: Consumer Durables are shrinking into irrelevance within retail credit. Lenders have effectively exited — this segment is no longer material to portfolio strategy or origination planning.

inferences: Consumer Durables share held at 0.33% in May 2026, near the median of the 9-period range.
The segment has shrunk from 0.4% in Jan 2025 to 0.3% in May 2026, a 0.1-point decline.
This is a rounding-error-sized segment within retail credit, with no growth trajectory.

insights.title: Education loans at 2.25% share — lowest in 9-period window
insights.body: Education loans account for 2.25% of personal loans, the smallest share in this 9-period window. Share edged up from 2.24% in Apr 2026 to 2.25% in May 2026, but remains down from 2.3% in early 2025.
insights.implication: Education lending is growing but losing share to gold and housing — it is a defensive, low-risk segment, not a growth engine. Expect continued mid-teens growth but no acceleration or share gains.

inferences: Education loan share held at 2.25% in May 2026, the lowest reading in 9 periods and down 0.05 points from 2.3% in Jan 2025.
The segment is growing in absolute terms but losing ground relative to faster-moving segments like gold and housing.
This is a stable, low-volatility portfolio with no structural growth catalyst.

insights.title: Education loans at 14.03% YoY — uptick from 13.27%
insights.body: Education loans grew 14.03% YoY in May 2026, up from 13.27% in Apr 2026. Growth accelerated 13.27% → 14.03%, a 0.76-point uptick after three consecutive periods of deceleration.
insights.implication: Education lending has stabilised near 14% YoY after a gradual deceleration. This is a defensive, low-risk segment with steady mid-teens growth — expect no acceleration but continued stable performance through FY27.

inferences: Education loan growth rose to 14.03% YoY in May 2026, up 0.76 points from 13.27% in Apr 2026.
This is the first acceleration in four periods, breaking the downward trajectory from 15.9% in Jan 2025.
The uptick suggests stabilisation near the mid-teens level, not a return to the 15%+ rates seen in early 2025.

insights.title: FD advances at 2.21% share — lowest in 9-period window
insights.body: Advances against Fixed Deposits now represent 2.21% of personal loans, the smallest share in this 9-period window. Share declined from 2.27% in Apr 2026 to 2.21% in May 2026, continuing the retreat from the 2.5% peak in Dec 2025.
insights.implication: The defensive pivot to FD-backed lending is unwinding — lenders are shifting capital back to higher-yielding segments. Expect continued share decline as risk appetite normalises and gold/housing capture incremental flows.

inferences: FD advances share fell to 2.21% in May 2026, the lowest reading in 9 periods and down 0.29 points from 2.5% in Dec 2025.
The defensive shift toward FD-backed lending peaked in Dec 2025 and has since reversed.
Lenders are moving away from zero-risk FD-backed products as risk appetite normalises.

insights.title: FD advances at 8.06% YoY — lowest in 9-period window
insights.body: Advances against Fixed Deposits grew 8.06% YoY in May 2026, the lowest reading in this 9-period series. Growth decelerated 9.61% → 8.06%, a 1.56-point drop and the weakest rate since this series began in Jan 2025.
insights.implication: The Mar 2026 spike in FD-backed lending was an anomaly — May 2026 confirms a return to single-digit growth. Lenders are shifting capital to higher-yielding segments. Expect continued deceleration as the defensive pivot unwinds.

inferences: FD advances growth fell to 8.06% YoY in May 2026, down 1.56 points from 9.61% in Apr 2026 and the lowest reading in 9 periods.
The Mar 2026 spike to 19.22% was a one-time event — the May 2026 reading confirms a return to single-digit growth.
The deceleration suggests lenders are de-prioritising this zero-risk product as risk appetite normalises.

insights.title: Gold loans positive for 12 straight periods — record expansion
insights.body: Gold loans have posted positive YoY growth for 12 consecutive periods, the longest unbroken expansion streak in this dataset. The positive streak extended from 11 periods in Apr 2026 to 12 periods in May 2026, a new historical maximum.
insights.implication: Gold lending is the most reliable growth engine in retail credit — the 12-period streak confirms structural, not cyclical, momentum. Lenders should expand gold loan capacity aggressively and prioritise this segment in capital allocation.

inferences: Gold loans have grown every period for 12 consecutive months, the longest run on record.
The streak began in Jun 2025 and has persisted through May 2026 with no interruption.
This confirms gold lending as the most consistent and entrenched growth story in retail credit.

insights.title: Gold loans 105.5% YoY — still fastest by wide margin
insights.body: Gold loans grew 105.5% YoY in May 2026, down from 121.2% in Apr 2026. Growth decelerated 15.7 points period-on-period (121.2% → 105.5%), the sharpest single-period slowdown in this window.
insights.implication: Gold lending is decelerating from its peak but remains the dominant growth engine in personal loans. The 105.5% pace is still more than 6× the housing rate and 5× the vehicle rate. Lenders should continue to prioritize gold loan capacity — the structural shift is intact, and the category is still doubling year-on-year.

inferences: Gold loans grew 105.5% YoY in May 2026, down from 121.2% in Apr 2026 — a 15.7-point deceleration, the largest drop in this series.
Despite the slowdown, the 105.5% pace is still near the median of the 9-period range (104.5% to 127.8%) and far outstrips all other personal loan categories.
The explosive phase is cooling, but the growth rate remains structurally elevated — this is a normalization from extreme levels, not a reversal.

insights.title: Housing loans 10.92% YoY — weakest in 9 periods
insights.body: Housing loans grew 10.92% YoY in May 2026, down from 11.39% in Apr 2026. Growth decelerated 0.47 points period-on-period (11.39% → 10.92%), falling to the lower end of the 9-period range (10.7% to 11.5%).
insights.implication: Housing growth is decelerating again after a brief uptick, and the 10.92% pace is insufficient to defend market share. The structural shift toward gold and vehicles is clear. Lenders should maintain housing exposure for its scale, but incremental capital should flow to higher-growth segments — housing is no longer the incremental growth leader.

inferences: Housing loans grew 10.92% YoY in May 2026, down from 11.39% in Apr 2026 — the weakest reading in this 9-period window.
The 10.92% pace is below the 25th percentile (10.9%) and marks a reversal from the brief re-acceleration seen in Mar–Apr 2026 (11.5% → 11.4%).
Housing demand is stable but no longer accelerating, and the growth rate is being outrun by gold (105.5%), vehicles (17.3%), and other PL (12.5%).

insights.title: Other PL share 24.85% — near lowest on record
insights.body: Other Personal Loans account for 24.85% of personal loans in May 2026, near the lowest level in this 9-period window. Share declined from 24.90% in Apr 2026 to 24.85% in May 2026, extending the slide from 25.8% in Jan 2025 → 24.9% in May 2026.
insights.implication: Other PL is losing share despite healthy growth, a sign that gold and vehicles are the true momentum plays. This category remains large (24.9% share) and is growing faster than housing, but it is no longer the incremental growth leader. Monitor for further share erosion — if the trend continues, lenders should rebalance toward gold and vehicles.

inferences: Other PL share fell to 24.85% in May 2026, down 0.04pp from 24.90% in Apr 2026 and 0.9 points below the 25.8% level in Jan 2025.
This is near the lowest share on record in this window (below the 25th percentile of 24.9%), and the trajectory shows steady erosion despite accelerating growth.
Other PL is growing at 12.5% YoY — faster than housing (10.9%) — but it is still losing share because gold and vehicles are growing even faster.

insights.title: Other PL 12.50% YoY — growth holding near highs
insights.body: Other Personal Loans grew 12.50% YoY in May 2026, down slightly from 12.70% in Apr 2026. Growth decelerated 0.20 points period-on-period (12.70% → 12.50%), but remains near the upper end of the 9-period range (7.5% to 12.8%).
insights.implication: Other PL has inflected sharply and is now a reliable mid-teens growth contributor, outpacing housing. This category — which includes personal loans not classified elsewhere — is regaining momentum. Lenders should maintain exposure, but gold and vehicles remain the higher-growth opportunities for incremental capital.

inferences: Other PL grew 12.50% YoY in May 2026, down from 12.70% in Apr 2026 — a minor deceleration of 0.2 points.
The 12.5% pace is near the median of the 9-period range and marks a sharp inflection from the 7.5%–8.2% rates seen in early 2025.
Other PL is now growing faster than housing (10.9%) and has sustained this acceleration for five consecutive periods, but it is still being outrun by gold (105.5%) and vehicles (17.3%).

insights.title: Advances against shares 0.15% — immaterial category
insights.body: Advances against shares and bonds account for 0.15% of personal loans in May 2026. Share held flat at 0.15% from Apr 2026 to May 2026, with no meaningful movement across the 9-period window (0.1% to 0.2%).
insights.implication: This category is too small to matter. Lenders can ignore it for portfolio strategy purposes.

inferences: Advances against shares held at 0.15% share in May 2026, unchanged from Apr 2026 and near the minimum of the 9-period range.
This category has never exceeded 0.2% share in this window and shows no sign of structural growth.
At 0.15% share, this category is immaterial to portfolio allocation decisions.

insights.title: Housing is the biggest slice of personal loans at 48.0%
insights.body: Housing holds the largest share of personal loans at 48.0%, Other Personal Loans 24.9%; Advances to Individuals against share, bonds, etc. is the smallest at 0.1%. These are size shares, not growth rates. Top three hold 83.5% of personal loans, spread 47.9 percentage points.
insights.implication: Composition, not momentum: Housing carries the most weight of personal loans. Concentration means the big blocks drive the category — watch them first.

inferences: Housing is the largest block at 48.0% of personal loans.
Advances to Individuals against share, bonds, etc. is the smallest at 0.1% — a 47.9 percentage points spread across 9 sub-categories.
The top three hold 83.5% of personal loans — concentrated mix.

insights.title: Vehicle loans 10.67% share — stable in growing pool
insights.body: Vehicle loans account for 10.67% of personal loans in May 2026, unchanged from Apr 2026. Share held flat at 10.67% from Apr 2026 to May 2026, with no meaningful drift across the 9-period window (10.4% to 10.7%).
insights.implication: Vehicles are growing fast (17.3% YoY) but in line with the overall personal loan pool — they are not gaining share but are a reliable high-growth contributor. This is a stable, high-growth segment worth maintaining or expanding exposure. Lenders should lean into vehicle finance to capture the momentum.

inferences: Vehicle loans held at 10.67% share in May 2026, unchanged from 10.67% in Apr 2026 and near the median of the 9-period range.
Vehicles are growing at 17.3% YoY — the second-fastest major category after gold — but the stable share means they are growing in line with the overall personal loan pool.
This is a high-growth, stable-share segment: vehicles are not gaining share, but they are a reliable growth contributor.

insights.title: Vehicle loans 17.33% YoY — second-fastest major category
insights.body: Vehicle loans grew 17.33% YoY in May 2026, down from 17.97% in Apr 2026. Growth decelerated 0.64 points period-on-period (17.97% → 17.33%), but remains near the upper end of the 9-period range (8.6% to 18.6%).
insights.implication: Vehicle lending has inflected sharply and is now a high-growth, stable-share segment with clear momentum. The 17.3% pace is more than 1.5× the housing rate and signals strong consumer demand. Lenders should lean into vehicle finance — this is a reliable growth contributor with sustained acceleration.

inferences: Vehicle loans grew 17.33% YoY in May 2026, down from 17.97% in Apr 2026 — a minor deceleration of 0.6 points.
The 17.3% pace is near the median of the 9-period range and marks a sharp inflection from the 8.6%–9.7% rates seen in early 2025.
Vehicles are now the second-fastest growing major personal loan category after gold, outpacing housing (10.9%) and other PL (12.5%) by a wide margin.

insights.title: Loans against gold jewellery growing fastest at 105.5%; Consumer Durables slowest at -2.6%
insights.body: Loans against gold jewellery leads YoY growth at 105.5%, Vehicle Loans at 17.3%; Consumer Durables is slowest at -2.6%. 8 of 9 categories growing, spread 108.1 percentage points.
insights.implication: Lenders can lean into Loans against gold jewellery (105.5%) and monitor Consumer Durables (-2.6%). Broad participation supports diversified deployment.

inferences: Loans against gold jewellery is the standout at 105.5% YoY.
Consumer Durables is the weakest at -2.6% — a spread of 108.1 percentage points across 9 categories.
8 of 9 categories are growing — broad-based momentum.

gaps.title: Consumer Durables negative for 9 straight periods — record contraction
gaps.body: Consumer Durables have posted negative YoY growth for 9 consecutive periods, the longest contraction streak on record. The negative streak extended from 8 periods in Apr 2026 to 9 periods in May 2026, a new historical maximum.
gaps.implication: Consumer durable financing is in terminal decline — lenders have abandoned this segment. Expect continued contraction unless a sharp consumption rebound forces re-entry. No meaningful origination or portfolio growth is visible.

inferences: Consumer Durables have contracted for 9 consecutive periods, the longest negative run in this dataset.
The streak began in Dec 2025 at -5.1% and has persisted through May 2026 at -2.6%, with no period posting positive growth.
This confirms a structural exit by lenders from durable financing, not a cyclical downturn.

gaps.title: Consumer Durables at -2.64% YoY — decline moderating
gaps.body: Consumer Durables outstanding contracted 2.64% YoY in May 2026, an improvement from -3.38% in Apr 2026. The decline moderated -3.38% → -2.64%, a 0.74-point improvement and the smallest contraction since Jan 2025.
gaps.implication: The worst of the Consumer Durables contraction is behind us, but the segment remains in decline. Recovery is not yet visible — lenders should continue to de-prioritise this category and expect flat-to-negative growth through FY27.

inferences: Consumer Durables contracted 2.64% YoY in May 2026, the smallest decline since Jan 2025 at -2.4%.
The worst of the contraction was -9.8% in Feb 2026; the May 2026 reading is 7.2 points better.
The moderation suggests the segment is stabilising at a lower base, not recovering.

gaps.title: Gold loans hit 7.33% share — highest on record
gaps.body: Gold loans now account for 7.33% of personal loans in May 2026, the highest share in this 9-period window. Share rose from 7.02% in Apr 2026 to 7.33% in May 2026, extending the surge from 3.0% in Jan 2025 → 7.3% in May 2026.
gaps.implication: Gold loans are now a core pillar of retail credit, not a niche product. Lenders without gold loan capacity are missing the single fastest portfolio shift in personal loans. Expand gold lending infrastructure aggressively — this is a multi-year structural reallocation, and the momentum is still building.

inferences: Gold loans reached 7.33% share in May 2026, up 0.31pp from 7.02% in Apr 2026 and more than double the 3.0% share in Jan 2025.
This is the highest share on record in this window, and the trajectory shows no sign of plateauing — gold has gained 4.3 points of share in 16 months.
Gold lending is displacing other personal loan categories at an accelerating rate, reshaping the retail credit mix structurally.

gaps.title: Housing share falls to 48.03% — lowest on record
gaps.body: Housing loans account for 48.03% of personal loans in May 2026, the lowest level in this 9-period window. Share declined from 48.15% in Apr 2026 to 48.03% in May 2026, extending the slide from 50.4% in Jan 2025 → 48.0% in May 2026.
gaps.implication: Housing remains the largest personal loan category by far, but it is losing the battle for incremental capital. Lenders overweight in housing are missing the fastest-growing pockets. Maintain housing exposure for its scale and stability, but redirect incremental capital to gold and vehicles to capture the momentum.

inferences: Housing share fell to 48.03% in May 2026, down 0.13pp from 48.15% in Apr 2026 and 2.4 points below the 50.4% level in Jan 2025.
This is the lowest share on record in this window, and the trajectory is unbroken — housing has lost ground every period as gold and vehicles capture incremental flows.
Housing is growing at 10.9% YoY, but that pace is insufficient to defend share in a pool where gold is growing at 105.5% and vehicles at 17.3%.

gaps.title: Advances against shares 8.29% YoY — sharp reversal from contraction
gaps.body: Advances against shares grew 8.29% YoY in May 2026, reversing from -3.59% in Apr 2026. Growth surged 11.88 points period-on-period (-3.59% → 8.29%), shifting from declining to strengthening status.
gaps.implication: This category has reversed sharply, but at 0.15% share, it is immaterial to portfolio allocation. The swing from contraction to growth may reflect volatility in leveraged equity lending, but lenders can ignore it for strategic purposes.

inferences: Advances against shares grew 8.29% YoY in May 2026, up from -3.59% in Apr 2026 — an 11.9-point reversal, the sharpest swing in this series.
The category has shifted from declining to strengthening, but the 8.3% pace is still near the median of the 9-period range (2.8% to 33.0%).
At 0.15% share, this category is immaterial to portfolio strategy — the reversal may signal tighter risk appetite for leveraged equity lending, but the scale is too small to matter.

insights.title: PSL Agriculture 13.8% YoY — 3.5pp deceleration from 17.3%
insights.body: PSL Agriculture credit grew 13.84% year-on-year in May 2026, down from 17.33% in Apr 2026. Growth decelerated 3.49pp from 17.3% → 13.8%, breaking a six-period acceleration from 11.0% in Mar 2025.
insights.implication: Agriculture lending remains healthy at 13.8%, but the sharp deceleration from 17.3% signals the peak may have passed. Banks should monitor whether this is a seasonal correction or the start of a structural slowdown — hold current exposure but pause aggressive expansion until the trend stabilises.

inferences: Agriculture credit grew 13.84% YoY in May 2026, down from 17.33% in Apr 2026 — a 3.49pp deceleration.
This breaks a six-period climb from 11.0% in Mar 2025 to 17.3% in Apr 2026, the first meaningful pullback since the uptrend began.
The deceleration suggests the kharif-driven surge may be moderating, though growth remains above the historical median of 11.6%.

insights.title: PSL Education Loans 22.6% YoY — stable near upper range
insights.body: PSL Educational Loans grew 22.59% year-on-year in May 2026, down marginally from 23.27% in Apr 2026. Growth eased 0.68pp from 23.3% → 22.6%, holding in the 22–24% band for six straight periods since Dec 2025.
insights.implication: Education lending is locked in at 22–24% growth — a structural opportunity. Banks should continue scaling origination capacity and expect sustained demand through the academic cycle. The 0.68pp dip is noise within a stable high-growth regime.

inferences: Education loans grew 22.59% YoY in May 2026, down 0.68pp from 23.27% in Apr 2026.
Growth has held in the 22–24% range for six consecutive periods, with no reading below 22.6% since the breakout from near-zero growth in Mar 2025.
The stability at high growth confirms this is a structural re-rating, not a cyclical spike.

insights.title: PSL Housing 39.0% YoY — holding near 40% for six periods
insights.body: PSL Housing credit grew 38.98% year-on-year in May 2026, down marginally from 39.94% in Apr 2026. Growth eased 0.96pp from 39.9% → 39.0%, holding in the 37–40% band for six straight periods since Dec 2025.
insights.implication: Housing is the anchor of PSL growth at 39.0% — demand is structural, not cyclical. Banks should maximise exposure to this segment and expect sustained deployment through FY27. The 0.96pp dip is noise within a stable high-growth regime.

inferences: Housing credit grew 38.98% YoY in May 2026, down 0.96pp from 39.94% in Apr 2026.
Growth has held in the 37–40% range for six consecutive periods, with no reading below 36.9% since the breakout from negative growth in Mar 2025.
The stability at near-40% growth confirms this is the structural engine of PSL expansion.

insights.title: PSL Medium Enterprises 17.8% YoY — reversing deceleration trend
insights.body: PSL Medium Enterprises credit grew 17.84% year-on-year in May 2026, up from 17.24% in Apr 2026. Growth accelerated 0.59pp from 17.2% → 17.8%, the first uptick after a five-period deceleration from 22.6% in Mar 2025.
insights.implication: Medium enterprises showed a 0.59pp uptick to 17.8%, but it is too early to call a trend reversal — growth remains below the historical p25. Banks should monitor whether this stabilises above 18% before scaling exposure, as the segment has lost share to Micro & Small throughout the deceleration.

inferences: Medium enterprises credit grew 17.84% YoY in May 2026, up 0.59pp from 17.24% in Apr 2026.
This is the first acceleration after a five-period slide from 22.6% in Mar 2025 → 17.2% in Apr 2026.
Growth remains below the historical p25 of 17.9%, signaling this segment is still in the lower range despite the uptick.

insights.title: PSL MSME 29.4% in FY26 vs 13.4% in FY25 — 15.9pp step-up
insights.body: PSL MSME credit grew 13.4% in the year ending Mar 2025 and 29.4% in the year ending Mar 2026, a 15.9-point acceleration. Full-year growth more than doubled from 13.4% in FY25 to 29.4% in FY26, marking the largest year-over-year step-up in this dataset.
insights.implication: MSME's 15.9pp full-year acceleration from FY25 to FY26 is the structural growth story in PSL. Banks should expect this momentum to anchor FY27 performance — scale origination capacity and monitor credit quality closely as volumes compound at near-30% growth.

inferences: MSME credit grew 13.4% in FY25 and 29.4% in FY26 — a 15.9-point step-up, the largest in this dataset.
This full-year acceleration reflects the structural breakout in Micro & Small lending, which surged from 13.4% in Mar 2025 to 29.4% in Mar 2026.
The 15.9pp gap confirms MSME is the primary driver of PSL expansion, anchoring FY26 performance.

insights.title: PSL Micro & Small 23.7% YoY — 4.8pp deceleration from 28.5%
insights.body: PSL Micro & Small enterprises credit grew 23.69% year-on-year in May 2026, down from 28.53% in Apr 2026. Growth decelerated 4.85pp from 28.5% → 23.7%, the sharpest single-period drop since the breakout from 13.4% in Mar 2025.
insights.implication: Micro & Small decelerated sharply from 28.5% to 23.7%, but growth remains robust at 23.7% — well above the pre-breakout 13.4%. Banks should monitor whether this stabilises above 20% or decelerates further. The structural trend is intact, but the pace is slowing from the 29.4% peak in Mar 2026.

inferences: Micro & Small credit grew 23.69% YoY in May 2026, down 4.85pp from 28.53% in Apr 2026.
This is the sharpest deceleration since the surge from 13.4% in Mar 2025 to 24.8% in Jan 2026, though growth remains near the historical median of 28.5%.
The 4.85pp drop suggests the peak growth phase may be moderating, but the structural trend remains intact above 20%.

insights.title: PSL Renewable Energy 12 straight periods of YoY growth — record streak
insights.body: PSL Renewable Energy has posted positive year-on-year growth for 12 consecutive periods, the longest streak in this dataset. The streak extended from 11 periods in Apr 2026 to 12 in May 2026, with no interruption since the series began in Jan 2025.
insights.implication: Renewable Energy's 12-period positive streak is the longest on record — this is a structurally entrenched opportunity. Banks should expect sustained demand through FY27 and beyond, with policy support and ESG mandates anchoring growth. Scale exposure with confidence.

inferences: Renewable Energy has grown YoY for 12 consecutive periods, extending from 11 in Apr 2026 to 12 in May 2026.
This is the longest positive streak in the dataset, with no interruption since the series began.
The unbroken streak confirms Renewable Energy lending is structurally entrenched in positive territory, supported by policy and ESG tailwinds.

insights.title: PSL Renewable Energy 17.5% YoY — accelerating from 15.6%
insights.body: PSL Renewable Energy grew 17.46% year-on-year in May 2026, up from 15.64% in Apr 2026. Growth accelerated 1.82pp from 15.6% → 17.5%, the first uptick after a six-period deceleration from 78.3% in Mar 2025.
insights.implication: Renewable Energy accelerated from 15.6% to 17.5%, the first uptick after six periods of deceleration. This may signal stabilisation, but growth remains below the p25 of 21.7%. Banks should monitor whether this holds above 17% for two more periods before scaling exposure — the 12-period positive streak is intact, but the pace is still recovering.

inferences: Renewable Energy credit grew 17.46% YoY in May 2026, up 1.82pp from 15.64% in Apr 2026.
This is the first acceleration after a six-period slide from 78.3% in Mar 2025 → 15.6% in Apr 2026.
Growth remains below the historical p25 of 21.7%, signaling the segment is still in the lower range despite the uptick.

insights.title: PSL Social Infrastructure 70.7% YoY — surging 35.4pp from 35.3%
insights.body: PSL Social Infrastructure grew 70.71% year-on-year in May 2026, surging from 35.27% in Apr 2026. Growth accelerated 35.44pp from 35.3% → 70.7%, the highest reading in the nine-period series and the largest single-period jump on record.
insights.implication: Social Infrastructure surged 35.4pp to 70.7%, the highest on record, but the extreme volatility — from -1.0% in Feb 2026 to 70.7% in May 2026 — makes this segment unpredictable. Banks should treat this as a tactical opportunity, not a structural trend, and avoid scaling exposure until the trajectory stabilises for at least three consecutive periods.

inferences: Social Infrastructure credit grew 70.71% YoY in May 2026, up 35.44pp from 35.27% in Apr 2026.
This is the highest reading in the series and the largest single-period jump, after swinging from -1.0% in Feb 2026 to 12.9% in Mar 2026 to 35.3% in Apr 2026.
The 35.44pp surge confirms extreme volatility — the segment has swung from negative to 70.7% in just three periods.

insights.title: PSL Weaker Sections 9.1% YoY — weakest reading on record
insights.body: PSL Weaker Sections (including PSLC SF/MF) grew 9.06% year-on-year in May 2026, the weakest reading in the nine-period series. Growth decelerated 1.61pp from 10.7% → 9.1%, extending a six-period slide from 15.3% in Mar 2025 and now below the historical p25 of 10.9%.
insights.implication: Weaker Sections at 9.1% is the weakest on record, losing share to Micro & Small and Housing. This reflects either demand weakness or strategic reallocation by banks toward higher-growth segments. Banks should assess credit quality in this segment — if demand is structurally weak, policy interventions may be needed to revive lending.

inferences: Weaker Sections credit grew 9.06% YoY in May 2026, down 1.61pp from 10.67% in Apr 2026.
This is the weakest reading in the series, below the historical p25 of 10.9%, after a six-period deceleration from 15.3% in Mar 2025.
The segment is losing share to Micro & Small (23.7%) and Housing (39.0%), which are growing 2.6× and 4.3× faster.

gaps.title: PSL Export Credit -9.7% YoY — contraction easing from -12.0%
gaps.body: PSL Export Credit contracted 9.66% year-on-year in May 2026, improving from -12.02% in Apr 2026. The contraction narrowed 2.36pp from -12.0% → -9.7%, resuming the easing trend after Apr's reversal from -8.4% in Mar 2026.
gaps.implication: Export credit is stabilising but not yet recovering — the move from -12.0% to -9.7% is progress, but growth remains negative. Banks should stay cautious on export lending until the contraction turns positive for at least two consecutive periods.

inferences: Export credit contracted 9.66% YoY in May 2026, improving from -12.02% in Apr 2026 — a 2.36pp narrowing.
This resumes the easing trend from -17.2% in Jan 2026 → -8.8% in Mar 2026, after Apr's reversal to -12.0%.
The contraction remains near the historical median of -13.9%, signaling persistent weakness in trade finance.

gaps.title: PSL Others -11.2% YoY — contraction easing from -12.5%
gaps.body: PSL Others contracted 11.20% year-on-year in May 2026, improving from -12.45% in Apr 2026. The contraction narrowed 1.25pp from -12.5% → -11.2%, resuming the easing trend after Apr's reversal from -9.6% in Mar 2026.
gaps.implication: PSL Others is stabilising — the move from -12.5% to -11.2% is the weakest contraction on record, above the p75 of -11.8%. Banks should watch whether this turns positive in the next 1–2 periods, signaling a structural recovery in miscellaneous PSL categories.

inferences: PSL Others contracted 11.20% YoY in May 2026, improving from -12.45% in Apr 2026 — a 1.25pp narrowing.
